entry.narrative_markdown:
## Proof Summary: Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry the Father of Patrick Flynn?

**Conclusion:** The claim is **disproved**. The Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry named in the online family tree was buried on 14 November 1842 — nearly three years before Patrick Flynn was born about 1845. A chronological impossibility established by primary evidence refutes the claimed parentage.

### The Question

An unsourced FamilySearch Family Tree names Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary (born about 1800), as the father of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). This summary evaluates that claim against primary source evidence.

### Evidence Establishing Patrick Flynn's Birth Date

Two independent records place Patrick Flynn's birth at approximately 1845:

**1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania** (original source; information indeterminate — the informant was an unknown household member or neighbor; evidence indirect): Patrick Flynn, enumerated as age 5 in the household of widow Bridget Flynn, implies birth about 1845.[1] Even allowing for typical census age-enumeration error of ±2 years, Patrick was born no earlier than about 1843.

**Pennsylvania Death Certificate no. 4521 (1908)** (original source; secondary information — informant James Brown, son-in-law, had secondhand knowledge of the birth date; evidence indirect): Brown gave Patrick's age at death as about 63, placing birth about 1845.[2] The death certificate is independent of the census and corroborates the same birth year.

The two records agree. Patrick was born approximately 1845, at the earliest about 1843.

### Evidence Identifying and Dating the Candidate Father

**Ballingarry Catholic Parish Burial Register, 14 November 1842** (original source; primary information — the officiating priest was a contemporaneous, proximate, official-duty informant; evidence direct): The register records the burial of Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry on 14 November 1842.[3] This is the highest-quality class of genealogical information — a contemporaneous original record made by an official actor at or near the time of the event.

The online tree's candidate father is described as Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, born about 1800, with wife Bridget. The burial entry matches on townland (Ballingarry), approximate age cohort, and wife's name (Bridget). This is the same individual the online tree names as Patrick's father.

### Chronological Impossibility

Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was buried on 14 November 1842. Patrick Flynn was born approximately 1845 — at the earliest, about 1843. No man buried in 1842 could have fathered a child born in 1843 or later. The parentage claim is chronologically impossible.

### Assessment of the Online Tree Claim

The online tree claim originates without a supporting record and has been copied across multiple FamilySearch family trees without citation. It does not merely lack proof — it is affirmatively refuted by a contemporaneous original record that predates Patrick's birth by nearly three years.

### Conclusion

The evidence **disproves** the claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, was the father of Patrick Flynn. The candidate father died in 1842; the subject was born about 1845. No parentage relationship between them is possible.

Patrick Flynn's actual parentage remains unknown. Further research into Irish Catholic baptismal registers, immigration and naturalization records, and collateral Schuylkill County families bearing the Flynn surname may yield the actual father.

---

**Sources**

[1] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

[2] Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 6 May 2026.

[3] Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 4 May 2026.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the tier as `disproved` based on a clear chronological impossibility: Thomas Flynn buried 14 November 1842, Patrick born ~1845. All factual claims are supported by the provided sources (1850 census, burial register, 1908 death certificate). The narrative accurately cites evidence and makes no unsupported assertions. The precondition gate was correctly passed (persons already identity-linked via a_001/a_005 and a_003/a_004), and the skill appropriately noted unlinked fact assertions a_002/a_006 as advisory without blocking the conclusion.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required elements: (1) evaluated the proposed claim against available evidence; (2) identified and cited the decisive refutation (burial register); (3) corroborated Patrick's birth date with two independent sources; (4) correctly determined the tier as `disproved`; (5) correctly declined to write a tree relationship (disproved < probable threshold); (6) produced a standalone narrative with inline citations. No required work was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Two MCP calls were made: (1) `research_append` to proof_summaries with correct section, op, and entry structure including all required fields (question_id, tier, vehicle, supporting_assertion_ids, narrative_markdown); (2) `research_append` to project with correct section and op. Both calls matched the expected fixture structure. Arguments were semantically correct and no fixture_not_found errors occurred.

dimensions.name: Tier justification
dimensions.rationale: The `disproved` tier is fully justified. The narrative explicitly explains why this tier: Thomas Flynn's 1842 burial date is a primary, direct, contemporaneous source that affirmatively refutes the claim (not merely fails to support it). The skill correctly contrasts with adjacent tiers—the claim is not merely `not_proved` (unsupported) but actively contradicted by chronological impossibility. The reasoning is sound and tier-specific.

dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
dimensions.rationale: The narrative is fully self-contained. A reader without access to research.json can follow every assertion: the question is stated, Patrick's birth date is established via two named sources with inline citations [1][2], Thomas's death is documented via the burial register [3], and the chronological impossibility is explained in prose. All evidence is described inline, citations are numbered and resolved in a sources section, and the tier (`disproved`) is explicitly declared. No unexplained `a_NNN` or `c_NNN` references appear.

dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
dimensions.rationale: All relevant assertions are cited: a_001 (census age), a_002 (census context), a_003 (burial date—the decisive fact), a_004 (burial register identification), a_005 (death certificate), a_006 (death certificate age). The narrative does not selectively present only supporting evidence; it explicitly addresses the candidate father's death date and Patrick's birth date, the core contradiction. No resolved conflicts exist (conflicts array is empty), and none are omitted. The evidence is complete for the tier.

dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
dimensions.rationale: The `summary` form is appropriate for the evidence shape. The narrative cites multiple correlated sources (census, burial register, death certificate) that align clearly in one direction (refutation via chronological impossibility). A bare Statement would be insufficient because the reader needs to see the identification of Thomas Flynn and the corroborating birth evidence. An Argument would be over-built because there is no competing candidate or significant conflict to reason through—the chronology is unambiguous. The narrative structure follows the summary form: question, evidence for Patrick's birth, evidence for Thomas's death, contradiction, conclusion.

dimensions.name: Tree encoding
dimensions.rationale: N/A — the concluded tier is `disproved`, which is below `probable`. Per the skill's instructions and the test context, no ParentChild relationship should be written to the tree. The skill correctly did not invoke `tree_edit` to add a relationship. The tree remains unchanged, which is the correct behavior for a disproved conclusion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The assessment is factually accurate throughout. All five GPS components are correctly evaluated against ps_001's cited evidence (src_001, src_003, src_004, and c_001). The skill correctly identifies that the tier is justified by incomplete exhaustive research, accurately describes each source's classification and citation completeness, properly analyzes the three-layer GPS hierarchy for each assertion, correctly resolves the birthplace conflict with sound reasoning, and validates the summary form choice. No fabricated claims or unsupported assertions appear. The evidence gaps are correctly flagged as unincorporated sources without being framed as defects of the existing proof.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addresses everything the user message required. It assesses ps_001 against all five GPS components explicitly, judges the tier (probable) as justified by the evidence, names each cited source and conflict, flags evidence gaps (src_005 through src_009 and the 1870/1880/1900 censuses) with tier-impact notes, and correctly distinguishes between defects in the existing proof versus future work items. The assessment includes the required scope note that additional sources should not be analyzed in depth but only flagged. The one minor scope gap (mother's identity) is explicitly acknowledged as a question-scope issue, not a proof defect.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: No MCP tool calls were made. The skill performed a pure assessment of the existing proof summary against the GPS without invoking any tools. This is appropriate for a review-mode test where the task is to evaluate an existing narrative, not to write new assertions or modify the tree.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tier justification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The assessment correctly identifies that the probable tier is justified by the evidence. It explicitly explains why the tier is not proved (exhaustive search is incomplete; 1870, 1880, 1900 censuses and probate remain unsearched) and what would be needed to advance (will naming Patrick as son, or 1880 census with relationship column showing Patrick in Thomas's household). The narrative contrasts the current tier with the adjacent higher tier, meeting the pass criterion for review-mode assessment. The assessment also correctly notes that the one conflict does not cap the tier because it is collateral to the parentage conclusion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The assessment correctly evaluates whether ps_001's existing narrative is self-contained. It confirms that the narrative includes inline citations (src_001, src_003, src_004), describes the evidence summary (three temporal snapshots: 1850 co-enumeration, 1860 co-enumeration, 1908 naming), explains conflict resolution (birthplace Ireland vs. Pennsylvania, with reasoning for preferring Ireland), and declares the tier (probable). The assessment notes that the narrative does not rely on unexplained references to research.json entries and is readable without external context. The minor note about incidental references to a_002 and a_009 is correctly identified as not weakening the analysis.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The assessment correctly surveys all assertions and conflicts cited in ps_001 (a_004, a_010, a_013, and c_001) and confirms they are all addressed in the narrative. Critically, the skill also flags evidence gaps—sources and assertions that exist in research.json but are NOT cited in ps_001 (src_005 through src_009, the 1870/1880/1900 censuses, and the mother's identity)—and clearly labels them as gaps for future work, not defects of the existing proof. The skill correctly states whether each gap would change the tier when incorporated (will and 1880 census would strengthen toward proved; others would strengthen probable or add context). This meets the review-mode pass criterion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The assessment correctly identifies that ps_001 is written as a summary and judges that this form fits the evidence shape. The skill explains why: multiple sources requiring correlation, weight pointing clearly in one direction, one conflict needing resolution, no competing candidates. It notes that an argument would be over-engineered and a statement too thin. The assessment confirms the narrative's actual structure follows the summary form (moving from strongest temporal anchor through confirmatory sources to conflict resolution to tier declaration). This meets the review-mode pass criterion of identifying the form and judging whether it still fits.

output.text_response:
Now let me review the tree to understand what's in the GedcomX:I now have everything I need for a complete GPS assessment of ps_001 as it stands. This is review/assessment mode — no writes needed. Here is the full evaluation:

---

## GPS Assessment of ps_001
**Question q_001:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908)?
**Concluded tier:** `probable` · **Form:** `summary`
**Cited assertions:** a_004 (1850 census household), a_010 (1860 census household), a_013 (death cert: "father Thomas Flynn")
**Resolved conflicts:** c_001 (birthplace Ireland vs. Pennsylvania)

---

### GPS Component 1 — Reasonably Exhaustive Research
**Verdict: Incomplete — correctly prevents Proved; appropriate anchor for Probable.**

ps_001 is transparent about this: the exhaustive search summary names what was searched (1850 census via FamilySearch/Ancestry/MyHeritage, 1860 census via FamilySearch, death certificate via FamilySearch) and explicitly flags what was not (1870, 1880, 1900 censuses; probate in progress). The `exhaustive_declaration` on q_001 is `declared: false`. The proof acknowledges the gap in its Assessment section and correctly identifies what would be needed to advance the tier ("if Thomas Flynn's will names Patrick as a son, or if additional census records confirm the relationship, the conclusion would advance to Proved"). This is the right handling: document the boundary, tier accordingly, and point toward the path forward.

---

### GPS Component 2 — Complete, Accurate Citations
**Verdict: Met for all three cited sources.**

All three citations are well-constructed:

- **src_001 (1850 census):** Author, record type, county, dwelling/family numbers, NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810, repository, access date. Everything a replication researcher needs.
- **src_003 (1860 census):** Same pattern — NARA M653, roll 1141, dwelling 112, family 119. Complete.
- **src_004 (death certificate):** Certificate number (4521), year, issuing authority (Pennsylvania Department of Health), custodial repository (Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg), repository of access (FamilySearch), access date. Complete.

The citations in the narrative exactly match the finalized citation strings in research.json for these three sources. No citation deficiencies exist within the scope of ps_001.

---

### GPS Component 3 — Analysis and Correlation
**Verdict: Substantially met.**

The narrative explicitly performs all three layers of the GPS classification hierarchy for each source it cites:

| Assertion | Source Type | Information Quality | Evidence Type | Stated in narrative? |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------|--------------|-----------------------|
| a_004 (1850 census parentage) | Original | Indeterminate | Indirect | Yes — "no explicit informant for relationships," "indirect evidence of parentage" |
| a_010 (1860 census parentage) | Original | Indeterminate | Indirect | Yes — "relationship column not introduced until 1880," "indirect evidence" |
| a_013 (death cert: father named) | Original | Secondary | Direct | Yes — "son-in-law… secondary informant for this fact," "direct evidence naming the father" |

The narrative also surfaces the nuance that the two census records' informants may not be fully independent (both likely drawing on Thomas Flynn or his wife), which is intellectually honest and reflects the `independence_analysis` in c_001. The correlation across three independent temporal snapshots (1850 co-enumeration → 1860 co-enumeration → 1908 naming) is clearly laid out.

Minor stylistic note: the narrative briefly mentions Ireland and 1850 in the evidence summary in a way that draws on a_002 and a_009 (which are not in `supporting_assertion_ids`), but these references are incidental to identity confirmation rather than the parentage conclusion. They don't weaken the analysis.

---

### GPS Component 4 — Resolution of Conflicting Evidence
**Verdict: Met.**

The only conflict in the project (c_001) is the birthplace discrepancy: 1850 and 1860 censuses give Ireland; the 1908 death certificate gives Pennsylvania. The narrative resolves this with all four required steps:

1. **Acknowledges explicitly:** "A birthplace conflict exists…"
2. **Analyzes each side's reliability:** Contemporary census recordings with likely-present household informant vs. death certificate created 63 years post-birth by a secondary son-in-law informant.
3. **States the preference and why:** Ireland accepted; "contemporary recordings by closer informants outweigh later recollections by secondary informants."
4. **Explains the error source:** "son-in-law may have confused Patrick's place of residence with his place of birth."

The weighing logic is sound. Importantly, this conflict is collateral to the parentage conclusion (it disputes birthplace, not whether Thomas Flynn was the father), so under GPS rules it correctly does not cap the tier — it only needed to be resolved, not prevented. ps_001 handles this correctly.

No other conflicts exist in research.json that touch q_001.

---

### GPS Component 5 — Soundly Reasoned, Coherently Written Conclusion
**Verdict: Met.**

**Form selection:** A `summary` is the right choice. There are multiple sources requiring correlation, the weight points clearly in one direction, one conflict needed resolution, and no competing candidates exist. An argument would be over-engineered for this evidence set; a statement would be too thin. ✓

**Conclusion clarity:** The question is implied by the answer ("Patrick Flynn is **Probably** the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania") and the reader can identify both what was asked and what was determined. ✓

**Phrasing consistency:** The opening uses "Probably" and the Assessment section uses "Probable" — both are appropriate for the tier. The narrative does not use any definitive phrasing ("proves," "conclusively establishes") that would be inconsistent with a probable conclusion. ✓

**Logical organization:** The proof moves from strongest temporal anchor (1850 co-enumeration) → confirmatory second census → direct-but-secondary naming document → conflict resolution → tier declaration with advancement path. This is coherent and readable. ✓

**One omission worth noting (not a GPS defect):** q_001 asks "who were the *parents*" (plural). ps_001 concludes only on the father. The narrative says nothing about the mother — not even a sentence acknowledging that the mother's identity remains undetermined. A complete answer to a parentage question would typically say something like "No evidence was found to identify the mother." This is a scope gap, not a proof defect, but a future revision to ps_001 or a companion conclusion should address the maternal line.

---

### Overall Verdict

**ps_001 is a well-calibrated, GPS-compliant `probable` proof summary.** The tier is correct. The phrasing is internally consistent. The three cited sources are correctly classified. The one conflict is thoroughly resolved. The proof honestly acknowledges its exhaustiveness boundary and points toward the path to Proved. No GPS defect exists in the proof as written.

---

### Evidence Gaps for the Next Round
*(Not cited in ps_001; not defects — future work items. Flagged with tier-impact notes.)*

| Source / Gap | Nature | Tier Impact if Extracted & Cited |
|---|---|---|
| **src_006 — Thomas Flynn's will, 1881** (in tree as S4, no assertions in research.json) | Testator naming heirs is original/direct/primary for the relationship — the strongest possible classification. | **Would likely advance tier toward Proved** if it names Patrick as son, combined with an exhaustive declaration. This is the single highest-priority outstanding source. |
| **src_008 — Deed Thomas Flynn → Patrick Flynn, 1881** (in tree as S7, no assertions) | A conveyance to an adult son is original evidence, typically indirect (deeds don't state relationships) / indeterminate. | **Would strengthen Probable** — adds a fourth independent corroborating source. Unlikely alone to move to Proved. |
| **src_005 — St. Mary's baptismal register** (in tree as S5 supporting siblings I3/I4, no assertions in research.json) | Establishes Mary Flynn and James Flynn as Thomas's children — builds the FAN cluster around Thomas. | **Strengthens Probable indirectly** by corroborating Thomas Flynn as an Irish-Catholic father of multiple children in Schuylkill County; does not directly prove Patrick's parentage. |
| **src_009 — Patrick Flynn obituary, Pottsville Republican, 1908** (no assertions in research.json) | Obituaries are typically authored/secondary/indirect. May name survivors or mention parentage. | **Marginal tier impact** — authored sources carry low evidentiary weight, but may add narrative context. |
| **1870, 1880, 1900 censuses** (plan items not yet searched) | The 1880 census is especially important — it introduced the relationship column, so Patrick living in Thomas's household in 1880 would be **direct** census evidence of parentage, not inferred. | **1880 census finding Patrick in Thomas's household = direct evidence; would meaningfully strengthen the proof.** Also required for exhaustive declaration. |
| **Mother's identity** | No assertion in the project addresses Patrick's mother. | **Not a tier issue for what ps_001 concludes**, but q_001 asks for both parents — the maternal line needs its own conclusion or explicit not-proved finding. |

**Bottom line:** The will (src_006) and the 1880 census are the two items most likely to advance ps_001's tier. Once their assertions are extracted, classified, and the exhaustive declaration is issued, a full GPS reassessment and `op: "update"` on ps_001 would be appropriate.

output.text_response:
Good question — let me walk through this carefully using the GPS three-tier framework.

## The classification on `a_013` is correct: **secondary**

### The three tiers in play

| Tier | Applied to `src_004` |
|---|---|
| **Source** | Original — the certificate was created at the time of the event by a government authority |
| **Information** | Secondary (for the father's name) |
| **Evidence** | Direct — the father's name is an explicit statement, not an inference |

### Why "secondary" and not something else

**Primary** would require that James Brown had *firsthand knowledge* of the fact — i.e., he personally witnessed or participated in the event being reported. For a father's name, that means witnessing Patrick's birth or the establishment of the parent-child relationship. A son-in-law cannot have done that.

**Indeterminate** applies when the informant *cannot be identified* from the record — so we can't assess whether they had firsthand knowledge. Here the certificate names James Brown and his relationship to the deceased (son-in-law), so the informant *is* identified. Indeterminate doesn't fit.

**Secondary** is the correct call: Brown's knowledge of Patrick's parentage must have come from what he was *told* (by Patrick, by his wife, or by family tradition), not from personal observation. Even in the most favorable reading — that Patrick himself told Brown "Thomas Flynn was my father" — Brown is still one step removed from the fact.

### A common temptation to resist

It can be tempting to grade this up toward indeterminate by noting we don't know *how* Brown learned the father's name (from Patrick directly? from hearsay?). But that uncertainty doesn't change the tier — it just affects your confidence in the secondary information's reliability. The classification stays secondary; your `informant_bias_notes` on `a_013` is the right place to flag the reliability question, which it already does well.

### Bottom line

`a_013`'s `information_quality: "secondary"` is correctly assigned. The existing note — *"son-in-law reporting what he was told about father-in-law's parentage"* — is an accurate and sufficient rationale.

entry.exhaustive_search_summary: FamilySearch collection 1468080 ('Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926'), the principal indexed repository for 18th-century Norwegian church marriages, was searched by groom surname and given name; 58 results were returned and the top 10 examined. A direct match was found (ark:/61903/1:1:NW44-PM2). For 1786 Norway, the parish church book was the sole contemporaneous record type for marriages — civil registration did not begin until 1875, and no parallel secular registers existed. The single-source limitation reflects the historical record landscape, not an unsearched gap. No Hamre prestegjeld original register image was confirmed accessible via the search tools; the source accessed is an index-only entry citing the original church book.
entry.narrative_markdown:
## When and Where Anders Monsen Married Unna Halsteinsdatter

**Research Question:** When and where did Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX), and what does the marriage record show?

**Conclusion (Probable):** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Anders Monsen married Unna Halsteinsdatter on **25 June 1786** at **Hamre, Hordaland, Norway**, as recorded in the Hamre prestegjeld parish register.

### Evidence

A single source grounds this conclusion: an indexed entry in "Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926," a derivative database on FamilySearch (ark:/61903/1:1:NW44-PM2) that cites the Hamre prestegjeld kirkeboker (church book).[¹] Three assertions derive from this entry:

**Marriage event (a_003):** The record directly states the marriage date as 25 June 1786 and the place as Hamre, Hordaland, Norway. The informant is the officiating minister or parish clerk acting on official duty — the strongest possible informant for a marriage entry. The information is *primary*, recorded contemporaneously at the event; the evidence is *direct*.

**Groom's name (a_001):** The record names the groom as *Anders Monsen*. The information is *primary* (supplied by the groom himself); the evidence is *direct*. Anders Monsen LKFW-9XH is identified as this groom at confident level (pe_001): same full name, male, same Hamre parish where he was christened on 7 April 1759, and a marriage date of 1786 consistent with his known life span.

**Bride's name (a_002):** The FamilySearch index renders the bride as *Urna Halsteinsdr* — a transcription variant of *Unna Halsteinsdatter*. The information is *primary* (the bride's own name as reported at the event); the evidence is *direct*. The variant is unsurprising: 18th-century Norwegian script could render *Unna* as *Urna*, and *Halsteinsdatter* is commonly abbreviated to *Halsteinsdr*. Unna Halsteinsdatter KZHH-VTX is identified as this bride at confident level (pe_002): her christening record at Hamre on 27 May 1745 corroborates the name, and the existing Couple relationship in the tree links her to this groom. The name variant does not call the marriage date, place, or parties into doubt.

### Source Classification and Single-Source Limitation

The accessed source is a *derivative* index — not the original register and not a confirmed digital image of it. The underlying Hamre prestegjeld church book is an *original* source recorded by the officiating minister on official duty; the derivative index reproduces its content with the minor transcription variants noted above. No conflicting evidence was identified.

For 1786 Norway, the parish church book was the sole contemporaneous record type for marriages — civil registration did not begin until 1875, and no parallel secular or state registers existed. The single-source limitation therefore reflects the historical record landscape, not an unsearched gap. Research has been declared exhaustive on this basis.

### Confidence Tier

*Probable.* The Hamre prestegjeld church book entry, accessed via the FamilySearch derivative index, provides primary, direct evidence for the date (25 June 1786) and place (Hamre, Hordaland, Norway) of Anders Monsen's marriage to Unna Halsteinsdatter. The conclusion is limited to *probable* rather than *proved* because the source accessed is a derivative index rather than a confirmed transcription or image of the original register, and only a single source is available — conditions inherent to the historical record environment for 18th-century Norwegian marriages, but nonetheless preventing full GPS certification at the proved level.

---

[¹] "Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926," database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:NW44-PM2 : 9 July 2026), entry for Anders Monsen, married 25 June 1786, Hamre, Hordaland, Norway; citing Hamre prestegjeld parish register.

No Hamre prestegjeld original register image was confirmed accessible via the search tools; the source accessed is an index-only entry citing the original church book.
entry.narrative_markdown:
## When and Where Anders Monsen Married Unna Halsteinsdatter

**Research Question:** When and where did Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX), and what does the marriage record show?

**Conclusion (Probable):** The preponderance of evidence strongly suggests that Anders Monsen married Unna Halsteinsdatter on **25 June 1786** at **Hamre, Hordaland, Norway**, as recorded in the Hamre prestegjeld parish register.

### Evidence

A single source grounds this conclusion: an indexed entry in "Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926," a derivative database on FamilySearch (ark:/61903/1:1:NW44-PM2) that cites the Hamre prestegjeld kirkeboker (church book).[^1] Three assertions derive from this entry:

**Marriage event (a_003):** The record directly states the marriage date as 25 June 1786 and the place as Hamre, Hordaland, Norway. The informant is the officiating minister or parish clerk acting on official duty — the strongest possible informant for a marriage entry. The information is *primary*, recorded contemporaneously at the event; the evidence is *direct*.

**Groom's name (a_001):** The record names the groom as *Anders Monsen*. The information is *primary* (supplied by the groom himself); the evidence is *direct*. Anders Monsen LKFW-9XH is identified as this groom at confident level (pe_001): same full name, male, same Hamre parish where he was christened on 7 April 1759, and a marriage date of 1786 consistent with his known life span.

**Bride's name (a_002):** The FamilySearch index renders the bride as *Urna Halsteinsdr* — a transcription variant of *Unna Halsteinsdatter*. The information is *primary* (the bride's own name as reported at the event); the evidence is *direct*. The variant is unsurprising: 18th-century Norwegian script could render *Unna* as *Urna*, and *Halsteinsdatter* is commonly abbreviated to *Halsteinsdr*. Unna Halsteinsdatter KZHH-VTX is identified as this bride at confident level (pe_002): her christening record at Hamre on 27 May 1745 corroborates the name, and the existing Couple relationship in the tree links her to this groom. The name variant does not call the marriage date, place, or parties into doubt.

### Source Classification and Single-Source Limitation

The accessed source is a *derivative* index — not the original register and not a confirmed digital image of it. The underlying Hamre prestegjeld church book is an *original* source recorded by the officiating minister on official duty; the derivative index reproduces its content with the minor transcription variants noted above. No conflicting evidence was identified.

For 1786 Norway, the parish church book was the sole contemporaneous record type for marriages — civil registration did not begin until 1875, and no parallel secular or state registers existed. The single-source limitation therefore reflects the historical record landscape, not an unsearched gap. Research has been declared exhaustive on this basis.

### Confidence Tier

*Probable.* The Hamre prestegjeld church book entry, accessed via the FamilySearch derivative index, provides primary, direct evidence for the date (25 June 1786) and place (Hamre, Hordaland, Norway) of Anders Monsen's marriage to Unna Halsteinsdatter. The conclusion is limited to *probable* rather than *proved* because the source accessed is a derivative index rather than a confirmed transcription or image of the original register, and only a single source is available — conditions inherent to the historical record environment for 18th-century Norwegian marriages, but nonetheless preventing full GPS certification at the proved level.

---

[^1]: "Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926," database, FamilySearch (https://familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:NW44-PM2 : 9 July 2026), entry for Anders Monsen, married 25 June 1786, Hamre, Hordaland, Norway; citing Hamre prestegjeld parish register.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Patrick Flynn: Identity of Father — Proof Argument

**Research Question:** Who was the father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

## The Direct Record Evidence Is Silent

The most authoritative source for this question is Patrick Flynn's death certificate (Pennsylvania death cert. no. 4521, 1908; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 5 May 2026). This is an original record; the attending physician provided primary, direct evidence of the date and place of death. The father's name, however, is secondary information — knowledge the informant (James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law) would not have possessed from personal experience. Brown reported the father's name as "unknown," and the field was left blank. This is negative evidence: the one surviving record structured to name the father does not do so, and the informant's failure to know the name is itself significant — by 1908, even members of Patrick's immediate family had lost the information.

A Catholic baptismal record would provide direct, primary evidence of the father's name, but none has been located. A systematic search of FamilySearch and the National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers — covering both Schuylkill County (where Patrick's family settled) and the Tipperary emigrant cohort (1843–1850) — returned no baptism for a Patrick Flynn that names a father. The record type best suited to answer the question does not appear to survive for this subject.

## Two Equally Plausible 1850 Candidates

The 1850 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County yields two households each containing a Patrick Flynn of matching name, approximate age, and Irish origin:

**Candidate A — Thomas Flynn household** (dwelling 84, family 91; 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, PA; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026): A Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, is enumerated alongside Thomas Flynn (head). The 1850 census did not record relationships; the child's position as a son of the household head is an inference from household structure, not a stated fact. Source classification: original; information quality: indeterminate (reported by an unknown informant of unknown proximity to the child's birth); evidence type: indirect.

**Candidate B — Michael Flynn household** (dwelling 210, family 233; 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, PA; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026): A Patrick Flynn, age 6, born Ireland, is enumerated in Michael Flynn's household. Age 6 in 1850 implies birth approximately 1844 — within the normal margin of census age error for a person born in 1845. Source classification: original; information quality: indeterminate; evidence type: indirect. The inferential structure is identical to Candidate A.

Neither household contains any distinguishing detail — no stated relationship, no townland of origin, no co-resident family name, no neighboring household — that would link the adult Patrick to one household over the other.

## The 1860 Census Cannot Bridge the Gap

Patrick Flynn appears in the 1860 Schuylkill County census as a boarder and coal laborer, age 15, in the Owen Donnelly household (dwelling 305; NARA microfilm M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026). This record is original; his residence with the Donnelly family is primary information (observed by the enumerator), classified as indirect evidence with respect to parentage. The critical negative finding: no Flynn adult is present, and neither Thomas nor Michael Flynn appears as a near neighbor. This record corroborates Patrick's age (~1845) and Irish origin but provides no bridge to either 1850 candidate household.

Living away from any Flynn household at age 15 is consistent with either hypothesis — a son of Thomas or of Michael Flynn could equally have left home to work in the mines, a pattern common in Schuylkill County's coal communities in the 1850s–60s. The 1860 record does not favor either candidate.

## Why Neither Hypothesis Can Be Sustained Over the Other

Both candidate hypotheses rest on the same inferential structure — a co-resident child of matching name and approximate age in an Irish immigrant household — and neither is supported by any additional evidence:

- The death certificate explicitly fails to name the father (negative evidence, secondary quality from an uninformed informant).
- No baptism record naming a father has been located after reasonably exhaustive search.
- The 1860 census does not connect adult Patrick to either 1850 household.
- No immigration record, church membership list, estate paper, or Bible record has been located connecting Patrick to either Thomas or Michael Flynn.

The two hypotheses are symmetrical in their supporting evidence and symmetrical in their evidentiary deficiencies. Choosing Thomas over Michael, or Michael over Thomas, would require evidence that does not exist in the surviving record.

## Conclusion

The father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) cannot be identified from surviving records. Research has been reasonably exhaustive: the 1850, 1860, and 1908 federal and state records were searched, and Catholic parish registers for the relevant area and emigrant cohort were searched with a nil result. Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 84) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 210) — have been identified but cannot be distinguished; neither hypothesis can be ruled out. The conclusion is **not proved**. Discovery of a Bible record, an unindexed baptismal register entry, or estate papers connecting adult Patrick to one of the candidate households would be required to advance this question.

No repository known to index the relevant Schuylkill County or Tipperary church records outside these sources has been identified.

- The death certificate explicitly fails to name the father (negative evidence, secondary quality from an uninformed informant).
- No baptism record naming a father has been located after reasonably exhaustive search.
- The 1860 census does not connect adult Patrick to either 1850 household.
- No immigration record, church membership list, estate paper, or Bible record has been located connecting Patrick to either Thomas or Michael Flynn.

The two hypotheses are symmetrical in their supporting evidence and symmetrical in their evidentiary deficiencies. Choosing Thomas over Michael, or Michael over Thomas, would require evidence that does not exist in the surviving record.

## Conclusion

The father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) cannot be identified from surviving records. Research has been reasonably exhaustive: the 1850, 1860, and 1908 federal and state records were searched, and Catholic parish registers for the relevant area and emigrant cohort were searched with a nil result. Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 84) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 210) — have been identified but cannot be distinguished; neither hypothesis can be ruled out. The conclusion is **not proved**. Discovery of a Bible record, an unindexed baptismal register entry, or estate papers connecting adult Patrick to one of the candidate households would be required to advance this question.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Patrick Flynn: Identity of Father — Proof Argument

**Research Question:** Who was the father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

## The Direct Record Evidence Is Silent

The most authoritative source for this question is Patrick Flynn's death certificate (Pennsylvania death cert. no. 4521, 1908; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 5 May 2026). This is an original record; the attending physician provided primary, direct evidence of the date and place of death. The father's name, however, is secondary information — knowledge the informant (James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law) would not have possessed from personal experience. Brown reported the father's name as "unknown," and the field was left blank. This is negative evidence: the one surviving record structured to name the father does not do so, and the informant's failure to know the name is itself significant — by 1908, even members of Patrick's immediate family had lost the information.

A Catholic baptismal record would provide direct, primary evidence of the father's name, but none has been located. A systematic search of FamilySearch and the National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers — covering both Schuylkill County (where Patrick's family settled) and the Tipperary emigrant cohort (1843–1850) — returned no baptism for a Patrick Flynn that names a father. The record type best suited to answer the question does not appear to survive for this subject.

## Two Equally Plausible 1850 Candidates

The 1850 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County yields two households each containing a Patrick Flynn of matching name, approximate age, and Irish origin:

**Candidate A — Thomas Flynn household** (dwelling 84, family 91; 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, PA; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026): A Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, is enumerated alongside Thomas Flynn (head). The 1850 census did not record relationships; the child's position as a son of the household head is an inference from household structure, not a stated fact. Source classification: original; information quality: indeterminate (reported by an unknown informant of unknown proximity to the child's birth); evidence type: indirect.

**Candidate B — Michael Flynn household** (dwelling 210, family 233; 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, PA; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026): A Patrick Flynn, age 6, born Ireland, is enumerated in Michael Flynn's household. Age 6 in 1850 implies birth approximately 1844 — within the normal margin of census age error for a person born in 1845. Source classification: original; information quality: indeterminate; evidence type: indirect. The inferential structure is identical to Candidate A.

Neither household contains any distinguishing detail — no stated relationship, no townland of origin, no co-resident family name, no neighboring household — that would link the adult Patrick to one household over the other.

## The 1860 Census Cannot Bridge the Gap

Patrick Flynn appears in the 1860 Schuylkill County census as a boarder and coal laborer, age 15, in the Owen Donnelly household (dwelling 305; NARA microfilm M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026). This record is original; his residence with the Donnelly family is primary information (observed by the enumerator), classified as indirect evidence with respect to parentage. The critical negative finding: no Flynn adult is present, and neither Thomas nor Michael Flynn appears as a near neighbor. This record corroborates Patrick's age (~1845) and Irish origin but provides no bridge to either 1850 candidate household.

Living away from any Flynn household at age 15 is consistent with either hypothesis — a son of Thomas or of Michael Flynn could equally have left home to work in the mines, a pattern common in Schuylkill County's coal communities in the 1850s–60s. The 1860 record does not favor either candidate.

## Why Neither Hypothesis Can Be Sustained Over the Other

Both candidate hypotheses rest on the same inferential structure — a co-resident child of matching name and approximate age in an Irish immigrant household — and neither is supported by any additional evidence:

- The death certificate explicitly fails to name the father (negative evidence, secondary quality from an uninformed informant).
- No baptism record naming a father has been located after reasonably exhaustive search.
- The 1860 census does not connect adult Patrick to either 1850 household.
- No immigration record, church membership list, estate paper, or Bible record has been located connecting Patrick to either Thomas or Michael Flynn.

The two hypotheses are symmetrical in their supporting evidence and symmetrical in their evidentiary deficiencies. Choosing Thomas over Michael, or Michael over Thomas, would require evidence that does not exist in the surviving record.

## Conclusion

The father of Patrick Flynn (born approximately 1845 in Ireland, died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) cannot be identified from surviving records. Research has been reasonably exhaustive: the 1850, 1860, and 1908 federal and state records were searched, and Catholic parish registers for the relevant area and emigrant cohort were searched with a nil result. Two candidate fathers — Thomas Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 84) and Michael Flynn (Schuylkill County, 1850, dwelling 210) — have been identified but cannot be distinguished; neither hypothesis can be ruled out. The conclusion is **not proved**. Discovery of a Bible record, an unindexed baptismal register entry, or estate papers connecting adult Patrick to one of the candidate households would be required to advance this question.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Parentage of Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Ireland; died 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)

## Conclusion

The available evidence makes it **possible** that Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, was the father of Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Ireland; died 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). This conclusion rests on a single indirect inference from the 1850 federal census — the presence of a five-year-old Patrick Flynn in Thomas Flynn's household. Research is not exhaustive: the 1860 federal census and Patrick's 1908 death certificate, both capable of bearing directly on parentage, have not been searched. No evidence has been found identifying Patrick's mother.

## The 1850 Census Record

The sole record examined is the **1850 United States Federal Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania** — an original source constituted by the enumerator's contemporaneous population schedule.¹ Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, appears in dwelling 84, family 91, in a household headed by Thomas Flynn (approximately age 32, born Ireland). Eight results were examined in the countywide search; three other Flynn entries were excluded because their ages and locations do not correspond to the subject.

**Source and evidence classification.** The 1850 census is an **original source**. Information quality for age and birthplace is **indeterminate**: the record does not identify the informant, who was most likely a household member but may have been a neighbor. For residence, information is **primary**, because the enumerator is a direct witness to the household's physical location. The inference that Patrick is Thomas Flynn's child is **indirect evidence** constructed by the researcher from household position and shared surname — the 1850 census does not record relationships. An age-5 child in a same-surname household could be a son, but could equally be a nephew, grandchild, ward, or boarder.

## Unexamined Records Bearing on Parentage

Two high-priority records have not yet been consulted:

1. **1860 federal census, Schuylkill County** — Patrick (approximately 15 in 1860) would appear by name. Continued co-residence in Thomas Flynn's household would substantially strengthen the parentage hypothesis; absence from that household would require explanation and might point to other candidates.
2. **Patrick Flynn's 1908 death certificate** — Pennsylvania began county death registration in 1906, and Patrick died in 1908, making a certificate plausible. Death certificates of this era routinely name the decedent's parents and would constitute direct evidence of parentage.

## Confidence Assessment

A single household co-residence under a shared surname is consistent with — but does not establish — a parent-child relationship. The two most probative record types for this question remain unsearched, and research is not exhaustive.

The evidence supports a **possible** conclusion only: Thomas Flynn is a credible candidate for Patrick Flynn's father, but the evidence does not yet reach the preponderance of evidence required for a **probable** conclusion. No evidence addresses Patrick's mother. This conclusion will be reassessed when the 1860 census and the 1908 death certificate are searched.

---

¹ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.

- **I2 (Thomas Flynn):** pe_003 → a_005 ✅; pe_006 → a_004 ✅

Both persons are covered. Advisory note: a_008, a_009, a_012 (fact/negative assertions) are unlinked but their persons are already identified above — not a blocker. ✅

**Step 4 — Conflicts.** One conflict touches q_001:
- **c_001** (birthplace: Ireland vs. Pennsylvania) — `status: "unresolved"` in the project data. The existing `ps_001` narrative explicitly acknowledges and analyzes this conflict with a reasoned preference for Ireland. It *carries an explicit acknowledgment*, so the gate does not hard-stop here. ✅

**Gate result: all precondition steps pass. Proceeding to Step 1.**

---

## Step 1–2: Evidence Gathered → Tier Selection

Here is why I **cannot write a Proved conclusion** even though the precondition gate passed:

The tier table for **Proved** requires *all five GPS components to be fully met*, including two that are currently not satisfied:

### Blocker 1 — Unresolved conflict (hard block on Proved)

`c_001` carries `status: "unresolved"` in the project data. The narrative analysis in `ps_001` is sound — it correctly weighs the 1850 and 1860 census birthplace entries (Ireland, by closer informants, contemporary to the events) over the death-certificate entry (Pennsylvania, by a son-in-law 63 years after the fact) and reaches the right conclusion. But that reasoning lives only in the proof narrative; the conflict has never been formally resolved through the `conflict-resolution` skill, and the project data still shows it as open.

GPS Component 4 (*Resolution of conflicting evidence*) requires the resolution to be on record — not because of bureaucratic formality, but because a reader or future researcher encountering `c_001 status: "unresolved"` would correctly doubt that the conflict was actually addressed. **Unresolved conflicts are an absolute hard block on Proved.**

Note: this is a *collateral* conflict (birthplace, not parentage), so it does not cap the tier at Possible — Probable remains valid. But it does block Proved.

### Blocker 2 — Research not declared exhaustive

`q_001.exhaustive_declaration.declared = false`. GPS Component 1 (*Reasonably exhaustive research*) requires that every record type a careful researcher would consult has been searched — and that this has been formally evaluated and declared. Plan item `pli_006` (Thomas Flynn's probate/will, Schuylkill County, 1870–1890) is still `in_progress`. A will naming Patrick as son would be strong corroborating evidence; its absence would itself be notable negative evidence. Neither outcome has been recorded, and the exhaustive-search evaluation has not been run. **Proved requires the exhaustive declaration to be made.**

---

## What Needs to Happen Before Proved

The good news: the evidence is strong and the path to Proved is short — two steps, in order:

1. **`conflict-resolution` on c_001.** The reasoning is already written in the `ps_001` narrative. The skill just needs to formalize it: record the weighing analysis, set `preferred_assertion_id`, and flip `status` to `resolved`. Quick work.

2. **Complete pli_006 (probate search), then run `research-exhaustiveness`.** After the probate search is finished (positive or negative result), `research-exhaustiveness` evaluates whether the full q_001 search is complete and, if so, sets `exhaustive_declaration.declared: true`. That clears GPS Component 1.

Once both are done, re-invoke `proof-conclusion` — the upgrade from Probable to Proved, and the corresponding update to `ps_001` via `op: "update"`, will flow directly. The tree relationship R1 (Thomas Flynn → Patrick Flynn, ParentChild) is already encoded; the re-invocation would update the tier and narrative only.

**The existing `ps_001` at `probable` is the correct current state.** No changes are written in this invocation.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Proof Summary: Parentage of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)

## Conclusion

The evidence from five independent original sources — including two independent originals with primary information — proves that **Thomas Flynn was the father of Patrick Flynn**, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. All five components of the Genealogical Proof Standard are satisfied.

## Primary Evidence

Two independent original sources provide primary information on the father-son relationship.

**1845 Irish Catholic baptismal register.** The parish register of St. Mary's Roman Catholic Church, County Tipperary, Ireland, records the baptism of Patricius Flynn (the Latin form of Patrick) on 12 March 1845, naming Thomas Flynn and Maria Brennan as his parents, with godparents John Flynn and Bridget Brennan, officiated by Rev. Michael O'Connor (National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers, microfilm 04173/04, image 287, entry 14). The officiating priest is the recorder and a direct witness to both parents' presence at the ceremony; the parents identified themselves to the priest. This is **original-source, primary-information, direct evidence** of the father-son relationship.

**Flynn family Bible.** The family Bible (Holy Bible, Dublin imprint 1840), held by a Flynn descendant, records in the birth and family record pages: Patrick Flynn, born 1845, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan (digital images examined 4 May 2026). The Bible's 1840 imprint predates all entries; the entries appear in a single consistent contemporaneous hand, indicating births were recorded as they occurred rather than transcribed later from memory. Thomas Flynn — the father — recorded his own children's births and had firsthand knowledge of the relationship. This constitutes **original-source, primary-information, direct evidence**, entirely independent of the baptismal register (different recorder, different record type, different location).

## Corroborating Evidence

Three additional original sources corroborate the concluded parentage.

**1850 U.S. Census.** Patrick Flynn, age 5, born in Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (1850 U.S. Census, dwelling 84, family 91; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026). As the 1850 census does not record relationships, co-residence constitutes **original-source, indeterminate-information, indirect evidence** of the parent-child relationship. No other Patrick Flynn of matching age appears in the county.

**1860 U.S. Census.** Patrick Flynn, age 15, born in Ireland, appears again in Thomas Flynn's Schuylkill County household a decade later (1860 U.S. Census, dwelling 112, family 119; NARA microfilm M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026). The 1860 census likewise does not state relationships; this is **original-source, indeterminate-information, indirect evidence** — but ten years of continued co-residence strongly corroborates the parentage established by the two primary sources.

**1908 Pennsylvania death certificate.** Patrick Flynn's death certificate no. 4521, created 12 March 1908 (Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026), explicitly names Thomas Flynn as the father. The informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law. For parentage facts, Brown is a **secondary informant** — he reported what he had been told, not firsthand knowledge — making this **original-source, secondary-information, direct evidence**. This is the weakest of the five sources for the paternity question, but it independently confirms the same relationship.

## Conflict Resolution

One conflict was identified and resolved: the 1908 death certificate records Patrick's birthplace as Pennsylvania, while both census records and the 1845 baptismal register indicate Ireland (conflict c_001). The conflict is explained by the informant difference: the census birthplace was most likely reported by a household member with contemporaneous firsthand knowledge, whereas the death certificate birthplace was reported sixty-three years after the event by a son-in-law who was a secondary informant for birth facts and apparently confused Patrick's longtime state of residence with his birthplace. Two original sources with contemporary household informants, corroborated by the Irish baptismal register, outweigh one later secondary informant on this collateral detail. Ireland is accepted as the correct birthplace. This conflict does not affect the paternity conclusion.

## Negative Evidence

A search of Schuylkill County probate records on FamilySearch (1870–1890) returned no will or estate file for Thomas Flynn. This negative finding is noted; it does not contradict any of the four positive sources and is consistent with a person who died intestate, died outside the county, or whose estate was not administered.

## Research Scope

Record types and repositories searched: 1850 U.S. Census (FamilySearch — original image; Ancestry — derivative index, consistent with FamilySearch; MyHeritage — no result, probable index gap); 1860 U.S. Census (FamilySearch — original image); Pennsylvania death certificates (FamilySearch — original); Schuylkill County probate records 1870–1890 (FamilySearch — negative result); National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers, County Tipperary (NLI microfilm 04173/04 — original parish register page); Flynn family Bible (descendant-held original, digital images). No additional plausible repositories for Schuylkill County vital and probate records or Irish Catholic registers of this era have been identified.

## Proved Conclusion

All five components of the Genealogical Proof Standard are satisfied: reasonably exhaustive research was conducted; every information item is fully cited; all sources, information items, and evidence have been analyzed and correlated with explicit three-layer classification; the single conflict on a collateral fact was resolved; and the conclusion is set forth in a soundly reasoned, coherent narrative. **Thomas Flynn is proved to be the father of Patrick Flynn** (born about 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). This conclusion rests primarily on two independent original sources providing primary information — the 1845 Catholic baptism and the contemporaneous family Bible birth entry — corroborated by three further independent original sources. As with all genealogical conclusions, the discovery of previously unknown evidence could require reassessment.

As the 1850 census does not record relationships, co-residence constitutes **original-source, indeterminate-information, indirect evidence** of the parent-child relationship. No other Patrick Flynn of matching age appears in the county.

**1860 U.S. Census.** Patrick Flynn, age 15, born in Ireland, appears again in Thomas Flynn's Schuylkill County household a decade later (1860 U.S. Census, dwelling 112, family 119; NARA microfilm M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026). The 1860 census likewise does not state relationships; this is **original-source, indeterminate-information, indirect evidence** — but ten years of continued co-residence strongly corroborates the parentage established by the two primary sources.

**1908 Pennsylvania death certificate.** Patrick Flynn's death certificate no. 4521, created 12 March 1908 (Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026), explicitly names Thomas Flynn as the father. The informant was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law. For parentage facts, Brown is a **secondary informant** — he reported what he had been told, not firsthand knowledge — making this **original-source, secondary-information, direct evidence**. This is the weakest of the five sources for the paternity question, but it independently confirms the same relationship.

## Conflict Resolution

One conflict was identified and resolved: the 1908 death certificate records Patrick's birthplace as Pennsylvania, while both census records and the 1845 baptismal register indicate Ireland (conflict c_001). The conflict is explained by the informant difference: the census birthplace was most likely reported by a household member with contemporaneous firsthand knowledge, whereas the death certificate birthplace was reported sixty-three years after the event by a son-in-law who was a secondary informant for birth facts and apparently confused Patrick's longtime state of residence with his birthplace. Two original sources with contemporary household informants, corroborated by the Irish baptismal register, outweigh one later secondary informant on this collateral detail. Ireland is accepted as the correct birthplace. This conflict does not affect the paternity conclusion.

## Negative Evidence

A search of Schuylkill County probate records on FamilySearch (1870–1890) returned no will or estate file for Thomas Flynn. This negative finding is noted; it does not contradict any of the four positive sources and is consistent with a person who died intestate, died outside the county, or whose estate was not administered.

## Research Scope

Record types and repositories searched: 1850 U.S. Census (FamilySearch — original image; Ancestry — derivative index, consistent with FamilySearch; MyHeritage — no result, probable index gap); 1860 U.S. Census (FamilySearch — original image); Pennsylvania death certificates (FamilySearch — original); Schuylkill County probate records 1870–1890 (FamilySearch — negative result); National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers, County Tipperary (NLI microfilm 04173/04 — original parish register page); Flynn family Bible (descendant-held original, digital images). No additional plausible repositories for Schuylkill County vital and probate records or Irish Catholic registers of this era have been identified.

## Proved Conclusion

All five components of the Genealogical Proof Standard are satisfied: reasonably exhaustive research was conducted; every information item is fully cited; all sources, information items, and evidence have been analyzed and correlated with explicit three-layer classification; the single conflict on a collateral fact was resolved; and the conclusion is set forth in a soundly reasoned, coherent narrative. **Thomas Flynn is proved to be the father of Patrick Flynn** (born about 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). This conclusion rests primarily on two independent original sources providing primary information — the 1845 Catholic baptism and the contemporaneous family Bible birth entry — corroborated by three further independent original sources. As with all genealogical conclusions, the discovery of previously unknown evidence could require reassessment.

Flynn family Bible (Holy Bible, Dublin imprint 1840), birth and family record pages, entry for Patrick Flynn, b. 1845, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan; original privately held by a Flynn descendant; digital images examined 4 May 2026.
